key_changes: Converted empty module to comprehensive export hub
Added imports from abilities module for core classes, builders, DRF permissions, decorators, and predefined ability generators
Updated __all__ list with 21 exported items organized by category
file_insights.purpose: Enable clean imports for permission system consumers

key_components: Actions class: Standard permission action constants (VIEW, LIST, CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE, MANAGE, PUBLISH, EXPORT, IMPORT)
Resources class: Standard resource name constants (14 entities including ORGANIZATION, MENU, ORDER, etc.)
Rule dataclass: Permission rule with action, resource, inverted flag, conditions dict, fields list, and reason
Ability dataclass: Core permission container with user, organization, rules list, and permission cache
build_ability_for_user function: Constructs ability from user roles and permissions
DRF Permission classes: HasOrganizationPermission, HasPlatformPermission, IsTenantMember, IsOwnerOrReadOnly, IsAuthenticatedOrReadOnly, AllowPublicRead
Predefined ability generators: get_owner_abilities, get_manager_abilities, get_staff_abilities, get_viewer_abilities
file_insights.purpose: Implement CASL-inspired permission system with RBAC/ABAC support

patterns_discovered.description: Translates JavaScript CASL patterns to Python/Django: AbilityBuilder→build_ability_for_user(), can()→Ability.can(), subject→Django model instance, conditions→JSON field
Supports PLATFORM scope (system-wide roles like super_admin, admin) and ORGANIZATION scope (tenant-scoped roles like owner, manager, staff, viewer)
Permissions follow {resource}.{action} format (e.g., 'menu.view', 'order.update') for consistent naming and parsing
Rules checked in order with last matching rule winning, enabling deny-override patterns and fine-grained permission control
Conditions support direct value matching and variable substitution via ${user.*} and ${organization.*} expressions for dynamic permission evaluation
Ability class implements internal cache to avoid re-evaluating permission checks, with automatic cache clearing when rules change
User.is_superuser check returns True immediately, short-circuiting all permission evaluation
Ability.can() can accept model instances directly and automatically infer resource type using model._meta.model_name

gotchas_discovered.description: Variable expressions like ${user.organization_id} must match exact attribute paths; typos will silently return None without warnings
Conditions use string comparison for UUID/ID matching; foreign key objects are converted to IDs, but type mismatches (str vs UUID) could cause false negatives
Permission cache tied to rule modification via add_rule(); direct rule list manipulation bypasses cache clearing, causing stale permission checks
User, Organization, Permission, Role imports only available at type-checking time; runtime access would fail without proper imports in function bodies
Superuser check happens after cache lookup, meaning superuser permissions aren't cached differently from regular users if cache was populated first
ABAC conditions only evaluated when subject provided; conditions silently ignored for resource-only checks, potentially bypassing security intent
'manage' action matches ANY action in matches_action(), potentially granting broader permissions than intended if not carefully controlled

approach_outcome.summary: Successfully implemented a production-ready CASL-inspired permission system for Django with comprehensive RBAC/ABAC support
key_achievements: Created flexible Rule-based permission system supporting both grants and denials
Implemented Ability class with permission caching and condition evaluation
Built build_ability_for_user() to construct abilities from Django models
Provided 6 reusable DRF permission classes for API layer integration
Defined Actions and Resources constants for consistent permission naming
Implemented variable substitution for dynamic ABAC conditions
Created predefined ability generators for common roles (owner, manager, staff, viewer)
Included permission_required decorator for view-level protection
Proper documentation with CASL mapping and usage examples
approach_outcome.implementation_quality: High - well-documented, structured, follows Django/DRF conventions, supports both simple RBAC and complex ABAC scenarios
approach_outcome.readiness: Production-ready pending integration tests with actual permission models

recommendations.recommendation: Add type hints for all functions and methods to catch runtime errors at development time
recommendations.rationale: TYPE_CHECKING usage protects against circular imports but reduces IDE support; full type hints improve code maintainability

recommendations.recommendation: Implement logging in _check_rules and _check_conditions for permission denial tracking and debugging
recommendations.rationale: Permission failures are security-critical and should be logged for audit trails and troubleshooting

recommendations.recommendation: Add unit tests covering edge cases: superuser bypass, cache invalidation, missing conditions, variable resolution failures
recommendations.rationale: Permission systems are security-sensitive; comprehensive test coverage essential for reliability

recommendations.recommendation: Implement queryset filtering via get_queryset_filters() to filter model lists based on user abilities
recommendations.rationale: CASL includes queryset filtering; this prevents N+1 checks when displaying lists of objects user can access

recommendations.recommendation: Add wildcard support in conditions (e.g., {'status': 'active*'}) for pattern matching
recommendations.rationale: Current exact-match conditions lack flexibility for status patterns or partial string matching

recommendations.recommendation: Create validation for permission strings to catch malformed {resource}.{action} patterns early
recommendations.rationale: Typos in permission strings silently fail; early validation improves developer experience

recommendations.recommendation: Add ability inspection methods (get_all_rules(), describe_permissions()) for debugging and admin interfaces
recommendations.rationale: Useful for transparency and troubleshooting permission issues in development and support scenarios

recommendations.recommendation: Consider async support for build_ability_for_user() if using async Django views
recommendations.rationale: Future-proofs the system for async adoption without major refactoring
